pi_items.source: Task description and task.json line 17 specify patient first name as 'Lucas'. setup_task.sh line 25 confirms patient creation with first_name='Lucas'.
Task description and task.json line 18 specify patient last name as 'Tremblay'. setup_task.sh line 25 confirms patient creation with last_name='Tremblay'.
Task description line 5 and setup_task.sh line 19 both specify DOB as 2024-03-15.
Task description line 5 specifies 'Vaccine 1: MMR (Measles, Mumps, Rubella)'. This is a verified vaccine type manufactured by Merck (M-M-R II).
All keywords are accurate medical names for the MMR vaccine components (verified via CDC vaccine terminology).

Task description line 5 and task.json line 23 specify this synthetic lot number. No web search found this in real vaccine databases (verified via CDC Lot Number registry and general manufacturer searches), confirming it is synthetic test data as expected.

pi_items.verified_value: Deltoid muscle is a correct injection site for MMR vaccine
pi_items.source: CDC Administering MMR Vaccine documentation confirms the deltoid muscle (central, thickest portion, 2-3 fingerbreadths below acromion process) is a recommended injection site. Task description specifies this site.

pi_items.verified_value: Subcutaneous (SC) is a valid route for MMR vaccine
pi_items.source: CDC documentation and Mayo Clinic sources confirm M-M-R II can be administered subcutaneously OR intramuscularly. Subcutaneous is one of two valid routes. Task specifies SC.
Deltoid is verified as correct site. Left arm designation is a common specification. Keywords are appropriate for EMR system matching.

pi_items.verified_value: Meningococcal C (Men-C) vaccine is a verified vaccine
pi_items.source: Task description line 5 specifies 'Vaccine 2: Meningococcal C (Men-C)'. CDC and Mayo Clinic confirm meningococcal vaccines (including Men-C) are approved and in use. GSK manufactures meningococcal vaccines including conjugate vaccines.

pi_items.verified_value: ["Meningococcal", "Men-C"] are correct; NeisVac is a brand name used by some manufacturers
pi_items.source: Meningococcal and Men-C are standard medical names. NeisVac is a registered brand name for meningococcal C vaccines. Task keywords are medically accurate for EMR matching.

Task description line 5 and task.json line 30 specify this synthetic lot number. No web search found this in real vaccine databases (verified via CDC Lot Number registry searches), confirming it is synthetic test data as expected.

pi_items.verified_value: Vastus lateralis muscle in anterolateral thigh is correct for pediatric meningococcal vaccination
pi_items.source: Mayo Clinic and CDC documentation confirm vastus lateralis in the anterolateral thigh is the preferred injection site for infants and young children receiving meningococcal vaccine. Task specifies Right Leg/Thigh/Vastus Lateralis.

pi_items.verified_value: Intramuscular (IM) is the correct route for meningococcal vaccine
pi_items.source: Mayo Clinic, CDC, and drug.com confirm meningococcal vaccines are administered intramuscularly. Meningococcal vaccines (including Men-C) are standard IM vaccines. Task specifies IM.
Vastus lateralis is the medical term for the injection site. Right leg/thigh designation is standard. Abbreviations (RL) and keyword variations are appropriate for EMR system matching.

privileged_info_summary: Lucas Tremblay is a verified synthetic pediatric patient (DOB: 2024-03-15) created for OSCAR EMR testing. The task requires recording two immunizations: (1) MMR (Measles, Mumps, Rubella) vaccine via subcutaneous injection to the left deltoid muscle with lot number MMR4829B, and (2) Meningococcal C vaccine via intramuscular injection to the right vastus lateralis (thigh) with lot number MEN8821X. Both lot numbers are synthetic test identifiers. All clinical information is accurate: MMR can be administered subcutaneously or intramuscularly; deltoid is a verified injection site for MMR. Meningococcal C vaccine is administered intramuscularly, and vastus lateralis (anterolateral thigh) is the preferred site for pediatric patients. OSCAR is an open-source EMR system.
